does: This paper proposes a comprehensive framework for AI-driven Human-Autonomy Teaming (HAT) in tactical operations, synthesizing how machine learning techniques can augment human decision-making in complex environments. The framework addresses four core components: trust and transparency in AI systems, optimal function allocation between human and artificial agents, enhanced situational awareness through AI support, and ethical considerations for autonomous operations. Rather than presenting new algorithmic methods or empirical results, the work identifies and structures the key technical and organizational dimensions necessary for effective human-AI collaboration in tactical contexts, aiming to improve both operational effectiveness and safety.
connects: This work builds on established research in human-computer interaction, human factors in complex systems, and the growing literature on explainable AI (XAI) and interpretability. It responds to foundational work on function allocation between humans and machines, trust in automation and AI systems, and situational awareness in high-stakes domains like aviation and military operations. The paper engages with contemporary challenges in AI transparency that emerged from the machine learning community's recognition that black-box models pose risks in safety-critical applications. It also connects to ethical AI frameworks and governance discussions that have become central to responsible AI deployment.
enables: By proposing a structured framework, this work enables future researchers to systematically address gaps in human-autonomy teaming for tactical operations. It points toward development of practical metrics for measuring trust calibration and transparency in operational AI systems, design methodologies for adaptive function allocation under time pressure and uncertainty, and standards for ethical decision-making when AI recommendations conflict with human judgment or rules of engagement. The framework provides a foundation for interdisciplinary research combining machine learning, human factors engineering, and policy work, potentially facilitating better integration of AI tools into military and emergency response operations while maintaining human oversight and accountability.
